title: Have the model write the code once so deterministic work runs at CPU prices
summary: As GPT-5.6 keeps frontier intelligence expensive, Notion's Geoffrey Litt and Max Schoening argue for compiling a prompt into deterministic code once, then running it without re-invoking the model — the pattern Notion shipped as Workers.
takeaways: For repeatable, well-specified work, use the model once to write deterministic code, then run that code — stop paying inference prices to recompute an answer that never changes.
Frontier intelligence stays expensive and demand is near-infinite, so the durable cost win is architectural: turn a recurring inference bill into a one-time compilation cost.
Compile the stable parts to code and reserve model calls for genuinely novel work; Notion's Workers run the generated code deterministically at a fraction of the token cost.

sources.title: Notion podcast — pricing agent intelligence (Litt & Schoening)

body:
## What happened

On 2026-07-09, OpenAI's new flagship, GPT-5.6 Sol, [listed at five and thirty dollars per million input and output tokens](https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jul/9/gpt-5-6/) — frontier intelligence stays expensive while demand climbs. In a [Notion podcast](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oHmCqAQb7ho), Geoffrey Litt and Notion's Max Schoening frame the answer as "progressive compilation": have the model write deterministic code once, then run that code cheaply instead of re-invoking the model for the same work. Notion later shipped the idea as [Workers](https://www.notion.com/blog/introducing-developer-platform) — a hosted runtime for LLM-written code that is "deterministic and token-efficient."

## Why it matters

Re-running a model for work that never changes is paying inference prices for a cached answer. Treating code generation as a compilation layer over "English as the program" turns a recurring per-call bill into a one-time cost — the cheapest intelligence is the intelligence you don't re-run.

## How it works

1. **Intelligence designs; code executes.** Use the model once to produce a deterministic artifact, then run the artifact with no further model calls. A recent [compiled-AI paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05150) reports roughly fifty-seven times fewer tokens at a thousand transactions.
2. **Compile the stable parts.** Recurring, well-specified steps become code; only genuinely novel work re-invokes the model.
3. **Run it cheap and predictable.** Notion's Workers execute the generated code in a sandbox for "a fraction of the token cost," the same way every time.

> For work that doesn't change, the cheapest intelligence is the intelligence you never re-run.

## The catch

"Progressive compilation" and "intelligence per second per dollar" are the podcast's own framings rather than established terms — what's durable is the compile-once, run-many pattern, and it only pays off past a break-even (the compiled-AI paper puts it near seventeen transactions). It also assumes the task is stable enough to compile; anything that needs fresh judgment on every call still pays the model each time.

[Autonomy, Cost & Control](/guide/autonomy-and-cost/)
